summary.text: A single address appearance: the address was seen in this transaction.
is the position of the tx within its block — feed
Chunks that could not be fetched or parsed. Empty = fully complete.
A half-open... no — an INCLUSIVE block range
A parsed bloom filter: the raw bytes plus the decoded bloom count.
The whole file, retained for lazy bit reads during membership tests.
Number of adaptive sub-filters in this file.

Content cache keyed by CID. Both methods are async so consumers can
back it with the browser Cache API, IndexedDB, or disk. CIDs are
immutable content addresses, so entries never go stale.
A chunk that failed, with enough context to retry or report it.
CID of the file that failed (bloom or index).
Decoded chunk header: the two table sizes.
One chunk's metadata as it appears in the manifest.
IPFS CID of the bloom filter file.
Byte size of the bloom file (informational; bounds the fetch).
IPFS CID of the index chunk file.
Byte size of the index file (informational; bounds the fetch).
Inclusive block range this chunk covers.
Fetch a CID's bytes (cache-first), retrying + queueing as configured.
Max concurrent NORMAL (bloom) gateway requests. Default 6.
Extra attempts after the first failure. Default 1 (one retry).
Max concurrent PRIORITY (index-chunk) requests, in a SEPARATE pool from

. Index chunks are large (tens of MB); giving them their
own lane means a burst of bloom hits downloads chunks promptly without
stalling the ongoing bloom scan (and vice versa). Default 4.

Per-request timeout in ms. A gateway that accepts the request but
never responds (e.g. an unpinned CID it tries to resolve over the
DHT) would otherwise hang the whole query forever. On timeout the
request is aborted and treated as a failure. Default 20000.
Restrict to appearances within this inclusive block range.

Streaming sink: fires the moment a chunk's index is parsed and yields
one or more appearances, BEFORE the whole query finishes. Lets a UI
render (and hydrate) results as they arrive instead of waiting for the
full scan. Chunks resolve concurrently, so calls arrive out of block
order — sort on the consumer side. The final
still carries the complete, sorted set.
Called after each bloom/chunk step with a fresh progress snapshot.
The Unchained Index manifest — the root document for a chain's index.
Every chunk, ascending by block range.
Index build parameters. Passed through verbatim; not interpreted here.
IPFS CID of the binary-format specification this index was built to.
Index build parameters carried in the manifest's
Present on newer manifests; whether chunks may be missing from the set.
Bloom files fetched + parsed so far.
Total bytes pulled from the gateway so far (blooms + index chunks).
Index chunks fetched + parsed so far.
Chunks in scope after block-range filtering.
Blooms that reported a possible match (index fetch warranted).

(fetched via the gateway fetcher) are native. URL-served
manifests and the contract-publication resolver are supplied via

so the zero-dep core never grows a second fetch path
or a viem dependency (the example wires the contract

Why a chunk could not be turned into appearances. Carried in the
reader's

array so a partial answer is never mistaken for a
complete one (the toolkit's no-silent-downgrade invariant).

The single index format version this reader understands. The chunk and
bloom binary layouts in

are pinned to it. Valve's
own pulsechain / pulsechain-v4 manifests and TrueBlocks mainnet all
publish this exact string today (verified 2026-06-12).

Why a chunk could not be turned into appearances. Carried in the
reader's

array so a partial answer is never mistaken for a
complete one (the toolkit's no-silent-downgrade invariant).
The bloom or index file could not be fetched from the gateway.
The fetched bytes did not parse (bad magic, truncated, etc.).
Parse an address string (with or without

, any case) into its 20
raw bytes. Throws on anything that is not exactly 40 hex characters.

in this
chunk, in stored order. Returns

when the address is absent. The
address table is binary-searched; only the matched address's
appearance slice is decoded.
Render 20 bytes as a lowercase 0x-prefixed address.

— i.e. its high 18 bytes are all zero.
This is the precompile / reserved range (0x0…0000–0x0…ffff). chifra and
the Unchained Index do NOT index these, so querying one is meaningless;
worse, a near-zero bit pattern triggers bloom false-positives in nearly
every chunk. Treat as invalid in both the UI and any server.

means the address MAY appear in the
corresponding index chunk (fetch + parse it to be sure);

means
it definitely does not (skip the chunk entirely).
Normalize any accepted address form to lowercase 0x-prefixed.
Validate the header and decode the bloom count. Does not copy the body.
Validate the magic number and read the address / appearance counts.
Validate and decode an Unchained Index manifest.
